text:
The common successful entry point within all operations was an email message targeted to victim's public-facing services that contained a Microsoft Word document attachment.

Upon opening the attachment multiple malicious files were created or downloaded allowing the attackers to gain some level of access into the victim's infrastructure.

Data from section "last" is written into registry perhaps to keep track of last executed command.

Persistence was achieved by utilizing scheduled tasks.

Several of the Operating System's auto-start locations allowed attackers to gain Domain or even Enterprise Admin access.

Cloud services such as Google Docs and Google Forms were involved allowing attackers to keep track of infected systems, spread malware and perform further malicious activities.

Scripting code (PowerShell, JavaScript, VBS).

What seems to be the core tools of these activities is a variant of Anunak, remote backdoor, along with a Visual Basic Script specially crafted with data exfiltration features.

One other significant finding is that some of the executables were signed using valid certificates from Comodo, a Certification Authority.

text:
CARBANAK is a full-featured backdoor with data-stealing capabilities and a plugin architecture
key logging Capturing keystrokes to gather sensitive information
desktop video capture Capturing screenshots of the victim's desktop to monitor user activity.
VNC Using tools like VNC to remotely control the victim's machine.
file system management Managing and navigating the file system to locate files of interest.
file transfer Transferring files to or from a compromised system.
TCP tunneling Using techniques like TCP tunneling to encapsulate traffic and avoid detection.
OS destruction Destroying data on the victim's system to disrupt operations or cover tracks.
POS and Outlook data theft Stealing data from point-of-sale (POS) systems and Outlook.
Key logger Logs key strokes for configured processes and sends them to the command and control (C2) server
The command and parameter names are hashed before being compared by the binary, making it difficult to recover the original names of commands and parameters.
Desktop video recording Capturing screenshots of the victim's desktop to monitor user activity.
Downloads executable The act of downloading an executable aligns with this technique, which covers the transfer of tools or files into a victim's environment.
injects into new process The technique of injecting code into a new process is directly described by "injects into new process".
Ammyy Admin tool Ammyy Admin is a legitimate remote access tool often abused by attackers to gain remote control of victim systems.
Renders computer unbootable by wiping the MBR This involves the destruction of data structures, such as the MBR, to prevent the operating system from booting. This technique aims to render the system unusable.
Creates Adversaries may create user accounts to establish persistence, elevate privileges, or facilitate other malicious activities.
deletes Windows user account Adversaries may remove accounts to limit access to the system or to deny access to specific users, which can include deleting user accounts.
Deletes file or service Adversaries may delete files to remove artifacts and other traces of their presence from the host to avoid detection.
injects directly into new process The technique of injecting code into a new process is directly described by "injects into new process".
the desktop Takes a screenshot of Adversaries may use utilities to capture screenshots of the victim's desktop to gather information about the environment and operations on the victim's system.
sends it to the C2 server Sending data to the C2 server could utilize common web protocols to communicate, making the traffic blend in with legitimate web traffic.
Runs VNC plugin Using tools like VNC to remotely control the victim's machine.
processes to the C2 Returns list of running server monitoring and collecting information about the processes running on a system.
Download and execute shellcode from specified address Downloading and executing shellcode from a specific address
Messages are encrypted using Microsoft's implementation of RC2 in CBC mode with PKCS#5 padding Use of RC2 in CBC mode with PKCS#5 padding to encrypt messages.
The encrypted message is then Base64 encoded Use of Base64 encoding to represent encrypted data
replacing all the '/' and '+' characters Use of Base64 encoding to replace specific characters ('/' and '+') with others ('.' and '-')
CARBANAK communicates to its C2 servers The CARBANAK backdoor communicates with its command and control servers to exfiltrate stolen data
The encoded payload is then made to look like a URI by having a random number of '/' characters inserted at random locations within the encoded payload Described when the encoded payload is made to resemble a URI by inserting a random number of '/' characters in random locations. This is used to disguise the payload as a normal URI.
The malware then appends a script extension (php, bml, or cgi) with a random number of random parameters or a file extension from the following list with no parameters: gif, jpg, png, htm, html, php Described when the malware adds a script extension (php, bml or cgi) with a random number of random parameters or a file extension (gif, jpg, png, htm, html, php) with no parameters.
A 256-bit AES session key is generated and used to encrypt both message headers and bodies separately The CARBANAK backdoor's custom binary protocol uses RSA for the initial key exchange and AES for subsequent message encryption, providing an encrypted communication channel
All subsequent messages are encrypted with AES in CBC mode The CARBANAK backdoor's custom binary protocol uses AES in CBC mode to encrypt message headers and bodies, providing an encrypted communication channel.
CARBANAK's strings are encrypted in order to make analysis more difficult This technique involves making analysis more difficult by encoding or encrypting data and/or commands. It can be used to protect strings, file names, command-and-control (C2) server addresses, and other variables used by the malware.
the backdoor was embedded as a packed payload in another executable or in a weaponized document file of some kind CARBANAK has been delivered via weaponized document files and packed payloads in other executables, which can involve user execution of malicious files
the use of Power Admin PAExec for lateral movement use of PAExec

a spear phishing e-mail with a CPL attachment; although in other cases Word documents exploiting known vulnerabilities were used Exploitation of vulnerabilities in client software to execute arbitrary code.

After executing the shellcode, a backdoor based on Carberp, is installed on the system Transfer of tools or payloads from an external network to a compromised network.

Further forensic analysis took us to the point of initial infection: a spear phishing e-mail with a CPL attachment; although in other cases Word documents exploiting known vulnerabilities were used.

infected computers were used to record videos that were then sent to the Command and Control servers Collection of video from compromised devices.

Even though the quality of the videos was relatively poor, they were still good enough for the attackers, armed also with the keylogged data for that particular machine to understand what the victim was doing.

Attackers used spear phishing emails with malicious attachments Sending spear phishing e-mails with malicious attachments to induce the victim to execute the malware.

text:
the initial infections were achieved using spear phishing emails that appeared to be legitimate banking communications, with Microsoft Word 97 – 2003 (.doc) and Control Panel Applet (.CPL) files attached.
The email attachments exploit vulnerabilities in Microsoft Office 2003, 2007 and 2010 (CVE-2012-0158 and CVE-2013-3906) and Microsoft Word (CVE-2014- 1761) Exploitation of vulnerabilities in client software (e.g., document reader) to execute arbitrary code.
the shellcode decrypts and executes the backdoor known as Carbanak Using command and script interpreters to execute malicious commands and scripts.
Carbanak is a remote backdoor (initially based on Carberp), designed for espionage, data exfiltration and to provide remote access to infected machines
They then install additional software such as the Ammyy Remote Administration Tool, or even compromise SSH servers Attackers use remote services to move laterally in the compromised network.
Once the vulnerability is successfully exploited, the shellcode decrypts and executes the backdoor known as Carbanak.
video recordings of the activities of bank employees, particularly system administrators, were made Attackers capture video of the victim's system, often for surveillance or to collect credentials and other sensitive information.
The videos were sent to the C2 server Communications with the command and control server (C2)
the attackers abused the aforementioned services by impersonating legitimate local users who had the permissions to perform the actions later reproduced by the cybercriminals
install the same Carberp-like malware Transfer of tools or files from outside to the compromised system.
Carbanak contains an espionage component that allows the attackers to take control of video capabilities on the victim systems
The doc files exploit both Microsoft Office (CVE- 2012-0158 and CVE-2013-3906) and Microsoft Word (CVE- 2014-1761). Exploitation of software vulnerabilities to execute arbitrary code on the victim system.
The spear phishing email messages appeared legitimate and in some cases were sent from compromised coworkers´ accounts Using valid accounts to gain unauthorized access to systems.
Once the remote code execution vulnerability is successfully exploited, it installs Carbanak on the victim's system Transfer of tools or files from an external system to a system within the victim's environment.
Carbanak is a backdoor used by the attackers to compromise the victim's machine once the exploit, either in the spear phishing email or exploit kit, successfully executes its payload
Carbanak copies itself into "%system32%\com" with the name "svchost.exe" The attacker renames a malicious file with a legitimate name to avoid detection.
with the file attributes: system, hidden and read-only. The attacker hides files and directories by making them invisible to users and security tools.
The original file created by the exploit payload is then deleted. The attacker deletes files to eliminate traces of his activity and obstruct investigations.
To ensure that Carbanak has autorun privileges the malware creates a new service Creation or modification of a service to achieve persistence and automatic execution.
The naming syntax is "<ServiceName>Sys" where ServiceName is any existing service randomly chosen, with the first character deleted. Changing the names of files or processes to make them look legitimate.
Carbanak determines if either the avp.exe or avpui.exe processes (components of Kaspersky Internet Security) is running. Carbanak performs process discovery to identify specific processes associated with Kaspersky Internet Security (avp.exe or avpui.exe).
the malware gets the proxy configuration from the registry entry: [HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Internet Settings] The malware use registry keys to execute commands or to configure automatic loading at system startup.
Carbanak injects its code into svchost.exe. Carbanak injects its code into the svchost.exe process to mask its activities within the legitimate Windows process.
Carbanak downloads the file kldconfig.plug from its C2 server. Carbanak communicates with its C2 server to download the file kldconfig.plug, which includes the names of the processes to be monitored.
Carbanak logs keystrokes Carbanak records keystrokes on the infected system's keyboard to collect sensitive information such as credentials and other data.
takes screenshots every 20 seconds Carbanak captures desktop screenshots every 20 seconds to monitor user activity and collect additional sensitive information.
Carbanak sets Termservice service execution mode to Auto. Also, after executing this service Carbanak sets Termservice in automatic execution mode (Auto).
it modifies the executable code in memory in order to establish simultaneous work processes for both remote and local users Modifies executable code in memory to establish simultaneous work processes for remote and local users.
If Carbanak detects the banking application BLIZKO (funds transfer software) in the infected computer Carbanak detects the presence of BLIZKO
Carbanak is also aware of the IFOBS banking application and can, on command, substitute the details of payment documents in the IFOBS system Carbanak can manipulate payment document details in the IFOBS system on command.
Carbanak uses the HTTP protocol Carbanak uses the HTTP protocol to communicate with its C2 server.
with RC2+Base64 encryption, Carbanak uses RC2+Base64 encryption to encrypt data transmitted through the command and control channel.
Modules modified in this process are: termsrv.dll, csrsrv.dll, msgina.dll and winlogon.exe.
If Carbanak detects the banking application BLIZKO (funds transfer software) in the infected computer, it sends a special notification to its C2 server
There appears to be a preference for the Ammyy Admin remote administration tool for remote control This technique specifically covers the use of Ammyy Admin as a remote administration tool for controlling systems within a compromised network.
a Secure Shell (SSH) backdoor was used to communicate with the C2 server in 190.97.165.126 (operatemesscont.net). This technique involves the use of SSH for establishing unauthorized access to remote systems.
Metasploit,
PsExec
Mimikatz.
The video file naming conventions used the name of the application in the foreground (e.g., Outlook, Cmd, etc.) and only recorded user activity This technique involves capturing video of a user's actions on a system.

text:
contains an encoded Visual Basic Script (VBScript) The use of VBScript for executing commands or scripts on the victim system.

The RTF document we analyzed (SHA1 1ec48e5c0b88f4f850facc718bbdec9200e4bd2d) has an embedded OLE object contains a VBScript file The use of VBScript for executing commands or scripts on the victim system.

When the document is opened the targeted user is lured into double-clicking on the embedded object which is disguised as an image When the user double-clicks on the embedded object disguised as an image, the malicious script or executable is run.

Double clicking on the image results in a file open dialog for "unprotected.vbe". If the user executes this file then the malware will begin to execute. The user is tricked into double-clicking on the disguised embedded object, which leads to the execution of the malicious .vbe file.

The VBScript malware inside the RTF document (SHA1 cd75662751c59951717b4704ea2cdb6fb7ec19bc) is an encoded file. The VBScript is encoded within the RTF document, making it more difficult to detect and analyze.

The module is base64 encoded inside the main VBScript file The module is base64 encoded, which is a form of obfuscation to hide the malicious code from security tools and analysts.

The "ggldr" script will send and receive commands to and from Google Apps Script, Google Sheets, and Google Forms The "ggldr" script uses legitimate third-party web services such as Google Apps Script, Google Sheets, and Google Forms for C&C communication, hiding in plain sight within legitimate traffic.

Upon the first attempt to contact the hard-coded Google Apps Script URL with the user's unique infection ID, the C& that no spreadsheet currently exists for the user. Using legitimate third-party web services like Google Apps Script, Google Sheets, and Google Forms for C&C communication.

spear-phishing emails with malicious URLs Use of spear-phishing emails with malicious URLs

tainted documents Use of contaminated documents to download a Cobalt Strike beacon component.

Cobalt Strike

identify critical documents Discovery activities to identify and locate sensitive information and files within the compromised infrastructure.

prepare them for exfiltration Attackers prepare data to be exfiltrated from the compromised network to a location controlled by them.

malicious URLs Use of spear-phishing emails with malicious URLs

booby-trapped documents Use of contaminated documents.

Cobalt Strike

The spear-phishing emails sent to the financial institutions either end up with victims downloading a tampered document Use of contaminated documents to download a Cobalt Strike beacon component.

download the Cobalt Strike beacon

to exploit several unpatched Remote Code Execution Vulnerabilities and deploy a backdoor Exploitation of vulnerabilities to execute remote code.

Once the user attempts to open the attached documents, Execution of malicious files by the user.

scripts embedded within the files are dropped on the disk and automatically executed in the background. Scripts are embedded in the attached files and run automatically when the files are opened.

the attacks use reconnaissance tools designed to assess the state of the victim's workstation and determine what tools should be downloaded next Gather information about the system to evaluate the configuration and installed applications.

spear-phishing attachment Use of contaminated documents to download a Cobalt Strike beacon component.

16:48 – one of the employees opened the document within the spear-phishing email 16:49 – a second employee opened the same tainted document Execution of compromised documents by users.

Finally, a backdoor from the Command and Control Server was used to establish persistence in the infrastructure

download additional scripts Downloading additional scripts.

run shell commands to move laterally in the infrastructure, The attacker executes commands to interact with the system and move laterally.

delete files from the system Deleting files from the system.

cleaning registry keys to leave fewer forensics traces Attackers clean the logs to reduce the signs of their activities.

Credentials for one Domain Administrator were compromised and used throughout the duration of the attack Compromising the credentials of a domain administrator.

The"swift-fraud[.]com/documents/94563784.doc"URL was opened from the body of an email received in a spearphishing campaign targeting financial institutions. Use of spear-phishing emails with malicious URLs

The attack flow leaves behind a temporary file

after the opening of the original document file, downloaded from the malicious URL mentioned above Opening the original document downloaded from the malicious URL triggers the creation of the temporary file.

The document downloaded from the malicious URL introduces files into the victim's system.

KbhpQIcahFCuZwq.sct - (bb784d55895db10b67b1b4f1f5b0be16) This object was not found on disk, it was deleted after running by "MGsCOxPSNK.txt". The file "MGsCOxPSNK.txt" was deleted after execution to avoid detection and forensic analysis.

cqHfjCkTtMwG.doc (c2a9443aac258a60d8cace43e839cf9f) This file can be found at the path: C:\Users\[redacted]\AppData\Local\Temp\cqHfjCkTtMwG.doc. It is a decoy file, because it acts as a normal document file Use of decoy files that look like normal documents.

the malicious payload is being dropped Transfer and placement of the malicious payload on the system.

executed on the system Execution of the malicious payload through user interaction.

tCrrDqBQoCcEkbnK.txt (581c2a76b382deedb48d1df077e5bdf1) This object can be found at the path: C:\Users\[redacted]\AppData\Local\Temp\tCrrDqBQoCcEkbnK.txt. It is a configuration file for cmstp.exe. It downloads a DLL dropper from "cloud[.]yourdocument[.]biz/robots.txt" Transfer of malicious tools or files into the target system.

the role of decrypting and dropping yet another JavaScript Dropper on the system Obfuscation or encryption of files to avoid detection.

the DLL will self-delete. Deleting the DLL from the system to avoid detection.

the file was obfuscated and encrypted with RC4 Obfuscation of files to avoid detection.

the binary tried to download a JavaScript backdoor from the Command and Control server (C&C)"nl[.][redacted][.]kz/robots.txt"and saved the file to"%APPDATA%\9D01CA.txt" File transfer from a command and control (C2) server.

The file "9D01CA.txt"sent an initial fingerprint of the system compromised which contained the name of the antivirus solution installed on the system, the local IP address, username, computername and OS version The file "9D01CA.txt" collects and sends basic information about the compromised system, including computer name and operating system version.

text:
The crime group would send out bank employees spear phishing emails, impersonating legitimate companies, which harbored a malicious attachment.

If the attachments were executed, the malware is activated.

the criminals would use their malware to remotely control the victims' machines.

The group first launched a campaign in 2013 using malware known as Anunak to target financial transfers and ATM networks of financial institutions, but by the following year that malware had been improved into a more sophisticated version known as Carbanak.

From then onwards, the crime syndicate focused their efforts into developing an even more sophisticated wave of attacks by using tailormade malware based on the Cobalt Strike penetration testing software.

The spear-phishing emails contain attachments that eventually download and execute a dropper.

The backdoor is used to send system information and execute malicious code.

steals credentials.

screen recording.

the agent opened the attachment contained in the email The attacker uses social engineering to convince the agent to open a malicious email attachment. The email contains a malicious Word document. The email attachment was a malicious Word Document The agent executes the malicious attachment, triggered by the attacker. The malicious Word document contains the encoded .VBS script. that contained an encoded .VBS script capable of stealing system information desktop screenshots to download additional malware The malicious VB Script will use macros to search for instances of Microsoft Word running on the system This involves the use of Visual Basic scripts to execute commands on the system, which is what the malicious VB Script is doing.

The victim system will then reach out to http://95.215.47.105 to retrieve additional malware called AdobeUpdateManagementTool.vbs This technique involves transferring tools or files from an external system into the victim environment. The victim system retrieves the additional malware from http

Adds file to WindowsUpdate folder: vbs This technique involves transferring tools or files to the victim system. The malware adds the VBS file to the `WindowsUpdate` folder.

Adds persistence mechanism to the CURRENT_USER registry hive in the CurrentVersion\Run and CurrentVersion\RunOnce keys to autostart AdobeUpdateManagementTool. This technique involves adding registry keys to automatically execute programs upon user logon. The malware adds entries to the `CurrentVersion\Run` and `CurrentVersion\RunOnce` keys.

A scheduled task is created named SysChecks which calls the vbs This technique involves using scheduled tasks for persistence. The malware creates a scheduled task named `SysChecks` to execute the VBS script.

A service is created named 'ADOBEUPDTOOL' which calls the AdobeUpdateManagementTool.vbs This technique involves creating or modifying Windows services for persistence or to execute malicious code.

The malware drops a Shockwave Flash icon and disguises itself as such his technique involves disguising malicious files or processes to appear as legitimate ones.

The malware contacts the following and may attempt to download doc: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

This malware was capable of stealing significant system and network information Adversaries may attempt to get detailed information about the system they are targeting.

It was also used to download several other reconnaissance tools to map out the network. Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

it also downloaded additional malware that enables the next stage of the attack Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

could execute powershell scripts on command Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution.

Beaconing messages are sent out to 179.43.133.34 via standard HTTP GET requests every 5 minutes Adversaries may use web protocols like HTTP to communicate with their command and control (C2) servers.

The content of the GET request is encoded with Base64 and secondarily encrypted with RC4 Adversaries may use various methods to obfuscate data to evade detection.

Downloaded tools have included Nmap, FreeRDP, NCat, NPing, and others The malware downloads several reconnaissance tools like Nmap, FreeRDP, NCat, NPing to map out the network.

This malware executes a new iteration of svchost.exe and injects its malicious code into this running process Adversaries may inject code into the address space of another process to evade detection and achieve persistence.

It then drops a pseudo-randomly named configuration file into the %ProgramData%\Mozilla folder. This file's name is base64 encoded and based on the infected system's MAC code, so identifying it by name will be challenging. Adversaries may use masquerading to disguise their files or processes as something legitimate to avoid detection.

It then searches Kaspersky antivirus processes and terminates them if running on the victim system. Adversaries may disable or interfere with security tools to prevent them from detecting or responding to the attack.

it registers itself as a service with the following details: Service name: RpcSsSys (this name is random, may vary on different system) Adversaries may create or modify Windows services to ensure their malware runs automatically when the system starts or when a service is started.

steal local passwords Adversaries may dump credentials to obtain user passwords or hashes.

The malware searches Kaspersky antivirus processes and terminates them if running on the victim system.

install completely different remote desktop programs, such as VNC or AMMYY Adversaries may use remote access tools to control systems and maintain persistence.

It communicates via an encrypted tunnel on port 443 Adversaries use application layer protocols for command and control (C2) communications.

screen captures Adversaries may capture screenshots to gather information from the victim's desktop.

keylogger information Adversaries may capture keystrokes to gather information from the victim.

email addresses from the PST file Adversaries may gather data from local systems for exfiltration.

The file is OLE compound file format that contains an embedded .VBE (encoded VBS) script This technique involves the use of scripting languages such as VBS (Visual Basic Script) to execute malicious actions. The .VBE (encoded VBS) script is a form of scripting used to perform various actions.

stealing system information Adversaries may gather data from local systems for exfiltration.

desktop screenshots Adversaries may capture screenshots to gather information from the victim's desktop.

download / execute additional malware Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment.

When the malicious document file is opened, the embedded VBScript (VBE) file is dropped in the Windows %temp% folder. This technique involves configuring the system to execute the malicious payload during system boot or user logon. Although not directly related to dropping the VBScript, the placement in %temp% could be part of a persistence mechanism.

The dropped VBE file is a loader script that drops, installs and executes a second layer VBScript payload in the victim's system. This technique involves the use of scripting languages such as VBS (Visual Basic Script) to execute malicious actions.

It creates a folder named "WindowsUpdate_" in the Window's %temp% directory, If the folder already exists, it will create the folder in the parent directory where the script resides. This technique involves disguising files or processes to make them appear legitimate. The creation of the "WindowsUpdate_" folder and the naming conventions used may be attempts to blend in with legitimate system operations.

A registry key is created that points to the Loader's directory sh.RegWriTe"HKEY_CURRENT_USER\System\CurrentControlSet\Control This technique involves creating or modifying registry entries to ensure that the malware executes automatically upon system boot or user logon.

The second VBScript payload is embedded in the loader script as a Base64 string This technique involves encoding or obfuscating files or information to hide their true purpose. The use of Base64 encoding is a form of obfuscation that helps to evade detection by making the payload less recognizable.

The loader script decodes the base64 string This technique involves decoding or deobfuscating information to make it usable. The loader script decodes the Base64 string to retrieve the VBScript payload.

This is then saved to a file named "AdobeUpdateManagementTool.vbs" into the "\WindowsUpdate_" folder This technique involves transferring tools or files into a compromised system. The VBScript file is saved and created in the target system's directory.

A persistence registry key is also created by the loader script: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run Adversaries may achieve persistence by adding a program to a startup folder or referencing it with a Registry run key. These mechanisms ensure that the program runs each time the user logs in or the system starts.

The loader script adds a scheduled task with a task named "SysChecks". The purpose of this scheduled task is to run the payload script (AdobeUpdateManagementTool.vbs) in every 5 minutes Adversaries may abuse task scheduling functionality to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious code. This can be used to execute code at a specific time or on a recurring schedule.

A SWF icon file is also dropped in the folder in order to disguise the dropped file as a Shockwave Flash file Adversaries may change the file extension of a file to match a different file type. They can use this tactic to disguise files as benign or known types, making them less likely to be detected or scrutinized by security tools or users.

A shortcut file is also added in the Windows startup folder as "AdobeUpdateManagementTool.lnk" Adversaries may create or modify shortcut files (.lnk) that are automatically started at boot or logon to execute malware or other malicious code. This technique involves placing shortcuts in specific locations, such as the startup folder, to achieve persistence.

script uses obfuscation, a combination of base64 and integer-ed characters (chr) to hide malicious code The use of base64 encoding and integer-ed characters (chr) to obfuscate the script. This more accurately reflects the described obfuscation of the VBScript payload.

Steal system information System Name System Manufacturer System Model Time Zone Total Physical Memory Processor System Type Processor BIOS Version Networking information Computer name Domain User name Collecting information about the system, such as system name, system manufacturer, system model, total physical memory, processor details, BIOS version, and system type.

A powershell script (filename: screenshot__.ps1) is created The creation and execution of a PowerShell script.

to screenshot victim's desktop Adversaries may capture screenshots of the victim's desktop.

It may also be able to receive additional malware executables and install them on the victim's computer. The malware can receive additional executables from the attacker and install them on the victim's system. This technique involves transferring tools or files to a compromised system.

It can receive commands from the attacker to download and execute EXE files, VBScript, or Powershell script files. The malware can download and execute EXE files, VBScript, or PowerShell scripts, which aligns with the use of a backdoor to transfer tools or additional payloads into the victim's system.

The data is sent as a data encrypted with RC4 and Base64 It is sent via an HTTP POST tunnel to the attacker's server The data is encrypted with RC4 and Base64, which constitutes obfuscation. Obfuscation techniques are used to make it harder for security tools to analyze or detect the exfiltrated data.

screenshot__.ps1 - a powershell script that takes screenshots of the active desktop Powershell script

vb__.vbs - a VBscript sent by the attacker

ps1__.ps1 - a Powershell script sent by the attacker A PowerShell script.

it writes the cmdunig value to this registry key: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion If the registry key is used to ensure the malware runs on startup or after a logon, this technique applies.

Following is the System information sent to the control server when the command "INFO" is received from the attacker. OS Name Version Service Pack OS Manufacturer Windows Directory Locale Available Physical Memory Total Virtual Memory Available Virtual Memory System Name System Manufacturer System Model Time Zone Total Physical Memory Processor System Type Processor BIOS Version Computer name Domain User name Collecting information about the system, such as system name, system manufacturer, system model, total physical memory, processor details, BIOS version, and system type.

This system information is also stored in this registry key: HKEY_CURRENT_USER\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Network\CLM The malware interacts with the Windows registry, specifically modifying registry keys

The config filename is a base64 string comprising of a unique string and the MAC address of the infected system The configuration filename is a base64 string, indicating the use of obfuscation.

injects its code to that process he malware injects its code into a new `svchost.exe` process. Process injection is a common technique used to execute malicious code within the context of a legitimate process to evade detection and

it registers itself as a service with the following details: Service name: RpcSsSys (this name is random, may vary on different systems) The malware creates a new Windows service to ensure its persistence. It sets up the service with a path that disguises its true nature, often using a name that mimics legitimate services.

The malware checks for the "isDebugged" flag in the PEB (Process Environment Block). It also checks for significant delay of code execution by utilizing the GetTickCount() function. The malware checks the \"isDebugged\" flag in the PEB (Process Environment Block) and also checks for significant delay of code execution by utilizing the GetTickCount() function to detect if the process is being debugged

Strings are heavily obfuscated to avoid static string analysis Strings are obfuscated to avoid static string analysis

The malware enables the Remote Desktop by starting the Termservice service This technique involves using remote services such as Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) or other remote access tools. Enabling and configuring Remote Desktop is a direct use of this technique to maintain access to the system.

It also sets the service to auto-mode so that the service will start on Windows startup. Modifying the Termservice service to start automatically on Windows startup is a form of persistence where the malware ensures that it will be able to reconnect to the system after a reboot.

The malware utilizes the open source project called Mimikatz and reused codes from this project to steal clear text local passwords from Lsass memory dump. This technique involves extracting credentials from operating system memory, files, or other sources. Mimikatz is a well-known tool for credential dumping, and its use to extract passwords from LSASS memory falls directly under this technique.

$"  ammyy.pl- this enables the malware to run AMMYY remote desktop control software %"  vnc.pl- this enables the malware to run a remote desktop VNC application This technique involves the use of Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) for remote access to systems. While the text specifically mentions Ammyy and VNC, RDP is a common protocol for similar purposes.

ThemeterpreterDLL ismade operational via reflective DLL injection, which copies the meterpreter DLL into the memory space of a chosen victim process (such as explorer.exe) and runs it as a thread. dversaries inject code into the address space of another process to execute their payload. Reflective DLL injection is a method of process injection where a DLL is loaded into the address space of a process without the use of traditional Windows APIs.

ThemeterpreterDLL ismade operational via reflective DLL injection, which copies the meterpreter DLL into the memory space of a chosen victim process (such as explorer.exe) and runs it as a thread. Transferring tools or files to the victim's system. This encompasses the initial transfer of the Meterpreter DLL.

The {~random} file names are not really random, but instead a base64 combination of the victim's system volume serial number and MAC address, which are obtained by queries to the system's WMI Service Adversaries use Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to obtain information.

A PowerShell script created by the infected email attachment This technique involves using PowerShell to execute commands and scripts. The described behavior of using a PowerShell script to handle a base64-encoded payload is a common use case of PowerShell for malicious activities.

contains a base64-encoded block of text that is decoded, This technique includes various methods of obfuscating files or data to avoid detection. Base64 encoding is a common method of obfuscation used to hide the contents of a payload.

written into memory as a thread, and executed. This technique involves injecting code into the memory space of another process. The action of writing the decoded payload into memory and executing it as a thread aligns with process injection techniques.
